replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content: It wasn't just one philosopher, it was an entire cultural trend. The book you want is The Poisoned Embrace: A Brief History of Sexual Pessimism by Lawrence Osborne. He traces it to the Gnostics, but he also talks about the extent to which Greek culture generally went gaga over how awful sex was.

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
I did not see the thread you are talking about, but it would not surprise me if JMG had specifically been referring to Plotinus. He was sufficiently hostile toward matter that his student Porphyry wrote that he seemed almost embarrassed to have been born in a body. The Catholics lifted the idea that sex is only legitimate if it is for the purpose of producing children's straight from him--which isn't surprising, since St Augustine consider the Neoplatonists to be the closest to the Christians of any pagan philosophers, and quotes Plotinus directly in his Confessions.

More generally, everybody at that time was looking for an escape hatch from the material world. It is quite correct to say that the Christians did not get their attitude towards sexuality from Judaism. The Judaism of the time was a temple religion like any of the others, the only difference being that it focused specifically on the god of the Jews. The temple cults were not hostile toward the material world and things associated with it, like eating meat and having sex, but the Axial Age religions and philosophies that replaced them very much were. If Classical paganism had survived, its later form would honestly not have looked very different at all from Catholicism or Orthodoxy.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
It may not have been Plotinus specifically, but his attitude toward sexuality was more or less what you are asking about. Read Ennead 3, 5th tractate. And Plotinus hardly appeared in a vacuum-- his attitude toward sexuality was widespread, and part of a general rejection of worldly pleasures that often included abstention from wine and meat as well. And again, Plotinus's influence on Catholicism was direct and explicit via Augustine, and later indirect through Pseudo-Dionysius.

I think that it is more accurate to say that the Christianity of the first centuries AD was part of a broader current of rejection of materiality that you see in the Platonism, Hermeticism, Gnosticism, Manichaeism, Buddhism and really more or less every school of thought of that era. They really were sick of life on Earth and looking for an escape hatch!

Rather than thinking too much about it, though, I'd encourage you, if you want a relationship with Jesus, to get to know Him-- independent of churchmen and their opinions-- and see if He has the attitude toward human sexuality that he's supposed to. If He does, you'll know you need to find another deity. If not, well, no one needs to know what you do with your free time!

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
To be fair, that may have been a very natural pendulum swing away from where Greek culture had been previously, in its attitudes about sex (I expect to see a similar swing in western culture in my lifetime-- hold onto your hats!). Christianity stepped into a Greco-Roman world where sex was quite exploitative (whom you could have sex with depended on your social status, not anything to do with consent), and set out a set of radical new rules about it which many people-- particularly women and slaves-- found liberating. That, and the ability to cast out demons, were some of the main reasons Christianity caught on the way it did. The apostles weren't the cops showing up to break up a great party where everybody was having fun. They were throwing lifelines to people who had no worth in the culture they belonged to, and no right to their own bodies.

St. Paul does cast a dour light on it to the modern eye, but it's important to remember who he was writing for.

In Orthodox writing that comes after, you see a definite two-pronged approach. Monastics understand the very real value of abstention-- there is spiritual power in it, and it's worth pursuing. But you also find statements that endorse the rightness of sex, within the proper boundaries of marriage-- as an actual good, not a necessary evil. I recall reading some early church father, addressing gnostic-leaning ideas about the uncleanness of bodily needs, stating rather bluntly that a married couple who'd engaged in the nuptial embrace not only hasn't done anything wrong/unclean, they shouldn't even feel compelled to *wash* before they approach God in prayer because the act is holy, and they've done nothing to defile themselves.

I know a lot of this gets lost over the centuries via Catholicism and its protestant daughters. I'm not sure how the gnostic distaste for physical existence became so dominant in the west.

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
You're talking to an Orthodox Christian. "Ortho" = correct, "doxa" = belief/worship. We are all about that stuff!

There doesn't have to be a split between right belief and religious experience. That's kind of a red herring, IMO. Both are important, and neither is central-- That role belongs to repentance. You can have some beliefs that aren't exactly precisely correct and still devote yourself to God (we in fact have acknowledged saints who've had some of their writings declared heretical-- nobody's perfect). You can go your whole life without a religious experience and still devote yourself to God. But there is no salvation without repentance. "Right worship"-- all the varied and repeating practices of our faith-- is how we condition our mangled souls to the path of repentance. You might say, in ecosophian terms, we are following, and laying for others to follow, tracks in space ;) We would more likely say, perhaps, we are orienting ourselves toward God.

"Open to me the gates of repentance
O Giver of Life..."

What's funny is the common characterization of Christianity as a "religion of the book". Abrahamic faith, certainly. But we didn't derive our religion from the Bible, you know. Jesus didn't write it. The church, its teachings, its hierarchy, and its liturgical practice, came first. From all the heaps of writings and letters circulating among the various local churches, plus the Septuagint (the Greek version of the Hebrew scriptures that was in common use at the time), the church (which by then was more than three centuries old) compiled the Bible, for the use of the church. The Bible: a book compiled by the church, for use alongside, and in the context of, its liturgy, theology, disciplines, practices, hierarchy, etc. It's a living organism, all together.

I'm not denigrating Scripture but like Paul, the Bible too has a context. That context is the Church. You lose a lot by ripping it loose from that context. It's like ripping a leg from a living body. The idea that Christianity somehow derives from that book is... um... well, it's very common because Protestants, on ditching Catholicism, needed something to claim as authoritative. But it's always been a pretty weird snake-eating-its-own-tail kind of assertion. You can't re-create a living body from an amputated leg.

I can see how the insistence that "these are the official books, not those other books" would rankle with many of the commentariat here (some of whom are fond of writings that didn't make the cut), but at the time the canon of scripture was established, it was a necessary step to prevent continual controversy over a multiplying collection of writings (some of them pretty questionable, but not all condemned: just deemed non-essential like the protoevangelion of James) scattered over a multiplying number of churches and a very broad geographical territory, in an age of slow communications and laborious hand-reproduction of texts. It's not that we don't have plenty of other writings-- authoritative writings, even!-- it's that the Bible is a compilation of the ones deemed most essential by the church in the fourth century: the story of Christ, the incarnation, the resurrection, how salvation came into the world, and the first tottering steps of the infant church.

The important thing though, is that the Bible is a tool given to us to aid in our salvation. The Bible isn't God. The Bible isn't salvation. It's a piece of the organism that is the church, and isn't fully functional outside that context. it's only natural that if you are looking at it on its own, outside its context, there's going to be a lot that doesn't seem very relevant.

Well, as I see it, I'm not "trying to say something" about anything in my novels; I'm telling stories. Some of the characters in my stories fall in love, and some of them proceed from there to physical intimacy -- but not all, not by any means; there are couples like Laura and Owen, or like Brecken and Sho, who are deeply in love and express that in bed, but then you also have the relationship between Jenny and Justin, which is love in every sense of the word but the sexual, and there's the relationship between Miriam Akeley and Randolph Carter, which is very close but not at all sexual -- he's gay, she's the kind of woman who likes to spend time with gay men, and there you have it.

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
First century Judaism also had its own anti-sex factions within it. IIRC, the spread of this attitude began with the cultural Hellenism policy of Alexander the Great and his successor kingdoms. In addition to their subjects recognizing their political dominion over them, they wanted Greek culture to be adopted, too. The deuterocanonical books of the Maccabees has as its historical basis the Jewish armed resistance to this kind of policy, but some later works of the Hebrew/Old Testament canon nonetheless show the influence of this Hellenism.

If you want to read the primary sources for this kind of thing, you don't have to look much further than the writings of many prominent Stoic and Neoplatonist philosophers from the ancient world. Marcus Aurelius, Seneca, etc., have some choice words about human sexuality in their writings. One example is found in Marcus Aurelius's "Meditations," where he writes, "as for sexual intercourse, it is the friction of a piece of gut and, following a sort of convulsion, the expulsion of some mucus". Plenty of that to be found in ancient Roman and Greek philosophical writings.

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content: Something else occurs to me.  Essene priests also practiced celibacy.  John the Baptist was, if the Gospels are historically accurate, a priest.  He was also an ascetic, as is indicated by the camel shirt he wore and his sparse diet.  I've heard theories that John was an Essene, at least originally.  Jesus had some affiliation with his movement, since he was baptized by him.  So a mild asceticism that included celibacy could have been picked up by Jesus through his time with John, then later expanded upon by the general cultural milieu and its abhorrence of sexuality.  But in such a case, I'm not sure one can say that the early Christian attitude toward sex has no origin in the Judaism of the day.  A preference for celibacy and a mild ascetical attitude toward the body in general could have been first picked up from the Jewish Essenes and passed by John to Jesus, then to Paul and so on.  I don't know if that is actually the case, of course.

replies.title: Re: Roman celibacy philosopher
replies.content:
That's true but what you are referring to as traditional Judaism was not fully developed in Paul's time. The legal commentary to which you refer is in the Talmud. The Mishnah is based largely on oral traditions about what the Pharisees had taught while the Second Temple stood, but these traditions were not written down until the Third Century, by which time Christianity had completed its messy divorce from Judaism. Paul's letters and the first three Gospels were composed and written down earlier than the Mishnah.

AFAIK, the Sadducees in Jesus's lifetime were not anti-sex either.

Whatever sect it was that left some of their community rules in the Dead Sea scrolls, were male ascetics with purity rules that go beyond later traditional Judaism. Those people could have been Essenes. They might have been influenced by the Biblical legend of Samson. Hard to guess whether they were influenced by the flight from the physical world in the culture of late classical Paganism. They weren't mainstream Jews of the time, but they certainly were Jews.

replies.title: Dhyan/Chan/Zen
replies.content:
Fascinating bit of history I learned relating to Zen.

The original word is Dhyan. Dhyan translates as 'gently'. In the middle part of India it became Dhyana. In the southern part of India it became Dhyanan. But the northern part of India called it Dhyan. Bodhidarma took Dhyan to China. From there 'Dhyan' morphed into the word Chan. Japanese then morphed Chan into Zen.

Sadhguru has an article that goes into the history of Dhyan/Chan/Zen and how it acquired it's reputation.


https://isha.sadhguru.org/us/en/wisdom/article/zen-and-yoga

comments.title: Existential crisis
comments.content:
JMG, following your example I started to study astrology. So the first thing I did was decode my birth chart. It painted a very accurate picture of me. And now I'm in an existential crisis because of it.

All my worst and my best qualities, all my failings and my triumphs, that wasn't me, it's just planets playing with each other.

Where's me in all of this? I feel like I'm just an observer, a passenger in a car driven by someone else.

Now I understand the gnostics who defined planets as evil spirits that keep us trapped in the material world.

As Freddy Mercury sang: I want to break free!

Any words of wisdom, JMG?

replies.title: Re: Existential crisis
replies.content:
No, it's you. If somebody paints a portrait of you, are the features in the portrait just tubes of paint playing with each other?

You were born at the moment when you were born because you came to this life with a certain set of personal qualities, certain strengths and weaknesses, certain elements of good and bad karma. All those things are mirrored synchronistically in the moment of your birth, and thus in your birth chart. All those things, furthermore, are the results of your own actions in previous lives. You drove the car down the road that you now see through the windshield. If you want to go somewhere else, you can do so.

One other ppint: the stars incline, they do not compel. To the extent that you simply slide through life doing what seems natural, yes, the chart predicts your every action. To the extent that you make something unique and individual of yourself, the chart stops being a prison and turns into a springboard off which you can jump in any direction you choose. Now that you've studied your birth chart, you know the strengths and weaknesses you established in previous lives. What are you going to take from this life? That's entirely up to you.

replies.title: Re: Existential crisis
replies.content:
Ah, determinism. That was the first problem to demand my attention when I first set out to discover my own path in life. For me the initial problem was that of physical determinism, but it's interesting to see the idea of astrological determinism leading to basically the same problem.

I can't solve the problem for you, but I can give you the solutions I arrived at, which were two. The first was the intellectual recognition that "I" am not truly separate from the physical substances which make up my body—in your case, you are not truly separate from the planets playing with each other. "Thou art that," I suppose you could call it.

But that recognition alone doesn't solve the problem, because just as there is a sense in which you are the same as all things, there is also a sense in which you and they are separate, and so the feeling of being a mere observer still persists in some form.

And so to answer the question of "Where's me in all of this?" I had to get to know myself—to learn, through years of introspection, what my self is and how far it and its influence extends, so that I may distinguish what is me from what is not-me, and thus to gain some command over both.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Trad Catholic here:

a) Get a basic understanding of the Catholic Canon Law as outlined in the 1917 Code of Canon Law. I have some concerns with it, but it's a starting point. I'm not thrilled about the codification, but it retains most of the features of the old Canon Law. Read it, study it, and try to understand the worldview it espouses. It is very different from the Modernists, and grappling with that is a key element to being able to restore the older worldview.

b) Find others. One of your best sources is to find a Latin Mass, and attend it weekly, and volunteer in said church. A lot of them desperately need people, and this will help get something going, or free the time and energy needed for something else.

c) Prayer! One of the core teachings of Catholicism is that God has a plan, and so ask for his guidance on what can be done, and what needs to be done, and what would be a waste of time to even try. Also, one of the core things we lost was the Communion of Saints; the Community of the Church is not limited to the living. Find the Patron Saints of various things in your life, and start making a habit of prayer to them. Approach them as you would someone who's well known in the community to have a particular passion, and you'll find them very responsive and helpful. Pray the rosary, add those practices in which Catholics of the 19th Century did as a matter of course, and you'll find your world can change very quickly.

d) THROW OUT YOUR TELEVISION. Enough said.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Glad to hear it! Please, please note that these are fantasy novels and their magic is not the kind you can actually do in this world. Some of it's based on actual magic, but it's kicked up several notches. In the stories, a witch is a folk healer who knows some practical spells; an initiate is a member of one of the cults of the Great Old Ones who has some magical abilities as a result; a sorcerer or sorceress is typically a half-human child or descendant of one of the Great Old Ones and can shape the fabric of reality directly, in something like the way the Great Old Ones do.

2) There are no equivalents in the sense that nobody in this world can do the things that witches, initiates, and sorcerers can do in the stories. I drew the basic concepts, though, from magical practitioners in this world: witches are modeled on folk practitioners, initiates on members of magical orders such as the GD, sorcerers on the great legendary figures such as Merlin and Apollonius of Tyana. Nobody's modeled on Wiccans; my attitude toward Wiccans isn't all that different from Abigail Price's.

3) Thank you. There's good reason why he, and not Jenny, is the central figure of the series: I was bored to tears long ago by all the fantasy stories about Special People with Special Powers who are the only ones who can wield the Magic McGuffin and defeat Blorg the Bad, Evil Lord of Evilness, blah blah blah. I wanted the series to center on an ordinary guy: a military vet, reasonably tough and smart and brave but nothing special, who achieves greatness through his own capacities for love, loyalty, and plain ordinary guts.

Even the relatively unusual characters are also much more ordinary than your average fantasy characters. I was endlessly amused by one review of The Weird of Hali: Dreamlands that went on at some length about how weird it was that the main character of that novel, Miriam Akeley, was so -- so ordinary!!! Indeed she was, and that's the point: the kind of fantasy that insists that magic and wonder is only for Special People is to my mind actively harmful -- one more prop to the overinflated self-importance of the supposedly Special People in our current world.

replies.title: Re: Abraxas & Alt-catholics
replies.content:
1) It's an oversimplification to think of Abraxas as a purely evil power. In Gnostic thought he's the demiurge Plato discussed in the Timaeus, the maker of the world of matter; the numerical value of the Greek letters that make up his name add to 365, so he's the lord of time; the more stridently world-hating Gnostics saw him as evil, but a great many others saw things in less simplistic terms -- thus the signet rings and talismans, which acknowledge his rulership over matter. As for the Templars, they were Gnostics -- I'm pretty sure, as noted in a couple of my books, that they encountered some of the surviving Gnostic traditions in Palestine during the Crusader occupation, and ended up adopting a Gnostic view of Christianity, though not a hardcore world-hating one. (I suspect they were Naassenes or something very close to that, but that's really just a guess.)

2) You're most welcome!

3) The Old Catholic Churches are an international presence; they schismed over the (to my mind absurd) claim of papal infallibility put in place by the first Vatican Council in 1870. There are various different flavors of them. If they've maintained the old Mass in one of its proper forms, yes, that could help a great deal -- and if more people join them as the Roman church sinks into decadence, that could also help.

4) That's a subject about which I know very little. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Abraxas & Alt-catholics
replies.content: I'm interested in taking Gnosticism as my religion. What's a good starting point for that? What are the daily practices, if any?

replies.title: Re: Abraxas & Alt-catholics
replies.content: In both cases, that depends entirely on the Gnostic! There are a couple of dozen Gnostic churches out there, no two of which has the same teachings or basic practices, and a lot of unaffiliated Gnostics, ditto. Since the entire point of Gnosticism is gnosis, that is, personal experience of the divine, it's inevitably going to be quirky and eccentric. If you want to get into Gnosticism, read a good random assortment of books on the subject and do some web surfing with search strings such as "gnostic church," see what you find, and decide where you want to go from there.

It so happens that a large minority of human beings—up to a third, depending on the survey—report having at least one experience, at some point in their lives, that appears to involve contact with a disembodied intelligent being. Many of these experiences are spontaneous; others seem to be fostered by religious practices such as prayer, meditation, and ritual. Any number of causes have been proposed for these experiences, but I’d like to ask my readers to set aside the issue of causation for the moment and pay attention to the raw data of experience. There’s a crucial difference between the question “Does x happen?” and the question “Why does x happen?”—a difference of basic logical categories—and it’s a fruitful source of confusion and error to confuse them.

Whether they are caused by autohypnosis, undiagnosed schizophrenia, archetypes of the collective unconscious, the real existence of gods and spirits, or something else, these experiences happen to a great many people, they have done so as far back as records go, and religion is the traditional human response to them. If nobody had ever had the experience of encountering a god, an angel, a saint, an ancestor, a totem spirit, or what have you, it’s probably safe to say that we would not have religions. Human beings under ordinary conditions encounter two kinds or, if you will, worlds of experience: one that’s composed of things that can be seen, heard, smelled, tasted, and touched, which we might as well call the biosphere, and one composed of things that can be thought, felt, willed, and imagined, which we can call the noosphere (from Greek nous, “mind”). I had an encounter with one who was very lightly disguised--
My wife and I bought some wood planks that we needed for framing of concrete steps. I was working an evening shift that day, and it was the only day I could bring the wood home.
I tied it onto the roof rack of the car, and it blew off half-way home.
From nowhere, a contractor pulled over behind us. He was driving a brand-new, white pickup truck with a pole rack on the back that held several brand new ladders. He was wearing brand-new, spotless white painter's overalls, a white painter's hat also devoid of paint spots.
He immediately started helping us gather up the wood from the side of the highway--and he was really good at it! He re-tied the wood to the roof rack, a much better job than I did.

I tried to pay him in cash for helping us out, but he refused. "I just like helping people," he said, got into his truck, and drove away. Oh! And I forgot to mention, he had blonde hair and blue eyes.

Why would I think this was an angel?

1) My wife and I had a feeling about him.

2) He showed up at exactly the right place and time to help us.

3) I have never, before or since, ever seen a contractor in spotless coveralls with a spotless truck.

4) I have never, before or since, ever had a contractor who did a good job refuse cash payment.

5) He looked like a stereotypical 'angel in disguise' to a degree almost painful.

6) There was no company name, logo, or phone number on the truck. Pure white, and brand-new.

7) His demeanor was cool, calm, collected. No small talk, he just did what needed to be done.

Of course, there are other questions that will never be answered-- What did the angel do with the truck and the ladders? Can angels paint houses? Why did we get help then? I certainly don't get help from an angel every time I need help--In fact, embodied angel help almost never happens to me.
-- Tendentious Oracular Turtle

comments.content:
Good day Archdruid,

A bit of a burning question here....

I really don't get the essence or purpose of ancestor cultus/veneration in a modern Western context, despite how popular it's become among certain neopagans and a few occultists. It seems just so alien to me, and undoubtedly many other Westerners. Our culture today is so far removed from the ancients who engaged in these practices as a part of everyday life.

Given my (likely crude) understanding of reincarnation, I would have to ask why should one call out to a human soul that has most likely be reincarnated in at least several different bodies after its past incarnation someone attempting to venerate? I suppose back 2000+ years ago when human souls would have 500+ years downtime between incarnate lives this practice would have made a lot of sense. But today? It just seems like people doing something just because it was done in the past. Even if the spirit in question was still out of incarnation, don't they have their own problems to deal and thus not needing to be bugged by the living? with We know from the whole Spiritualism debacle that calling out to spirits very often gets the attention of some other spirit or group of spirits, and often undesirable ones.

My understanding of "the dead" largely comes from Dion Fortune's occult works on such matters and I'm finding these ideas really hard to square with the practice of ancestor veneration. She seems to have frowned on any practice akin to Necromancy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In societies that practice ancestor veneration -- I'm drawing here especially on Japan, since that's the source of the traditions I grew up with -- you only pray to individuals for a certain period after death, since they're assumed to have reincarnated after then. "The ancestors" collectively still receive regular worship, and so do those ancestors who are believed to have passed beyond incarnation as individuals.

I don't practice it myself, since most of my ancestors back quite a few centuries now were devout Christians and probably wouldn't be happy about being venerated! But it's an ancient and reasoned tradition, and if it's done with attention to the traditional lore, I don't think there's anything necessarily wrong with it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For my part, I began including ancestor worship as part of my practice as you say because "that's how it was done in the past", but as I've become more familiar with traditional western occult philosophy, I've had some similar questions (and I think even asked some of them here).

These days, I see it primarily as a means of recognizing the immense debt I owe to everyone who contributed to the world and life I live - yes, my actual body and genetic material and such, but also language, technology, law, culture, and so forth. As such, I generally offer prayers to my "forebearers" and sometimes I'll offer thanks to a particular subset (like, if I were praying to Tiw regarding justice, I might also offer thanks the forebearers who created the laws).

Another interesting perspective is Rune Rasmussen of Nordic Animism talking about the role of animal totems in linking humans to the natural world with a more active relationship: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vx5OEVDqRzI

As with the rest of my spiritual practice, I consider it very much a work in progress, but it seems to have been helpful to me so far.

Look forward to hearing anyone else's thoughts!
Jeff

0) Protective/cleansing prayer under the light of a blessed candle. Further prayer asking God for guidance and accepting whatever information he sees fit to communicate (even if not directly related to the question at hand).

1) Under the light of the blessed candle open the Bible at a random page. Extend my right palm over the left side, then the right side, trying to feel which is hotter, or if there's a slight tingling. If there's a noticeable difference, pass a few pages left or right, trying to zero in the correct page. Repeat as needed.

2)Fold the ring an pinky fingers, open the thumb. Pass the index and middle finger's tips over the page (not touching the paper), trying to detect which paragraph/line is hotter or produces a tingle. Zero in the correct versicle the same way you zeroed in the page before.

[As there's a candle involved, there would be a fire hazard to do either #1 or #2 with closed eyes, of turning your sight away. Instead, unfocus your sight so that the page is visible but individual words blur. Concentrate on the sensations of your palm and fingers instead of trying to guess which book or chapter you might be looking at]

3) When your gut tells you it is the right place, find out the book, chapter and versicle you are pointing at. Note it down first, then read it. This is short passage is where the core of the message is. For what comes next, when in doubt this is the core message.

4) Read the whole page for context. If you are not familiar with this book of the Bible, read some more ahead or before to gain further context, but don't overdo it. There are subtle clues in the page that let's you figure out how this relate to your own life, or with the question you intended to ask if that's the case, but the further you move, the easiest is to get lost in your own imagination.

comments.title: Etheric Goggles

Last week someone asked whether anyone had made the etheric goggles, and having attempted it, I thought I would reply. I found some resources that suggested that the various ZWB glasses were effective for this, so I ordered some welding goggles and some appropriately-sized ZWB2 filter discs. I must regretfully report that these do not work, at least for me. ZWB2 blocks most visible light, so almost nothing is visible indoors. Outdoors, once your eyes adjust, you can see the world around you as crimson trees against a blue-black sky, but no auras, at least not around trees, my hand, or squirrels.

NB If you try this, do not look at the sun with these filters. They let through near-UV and near-IR light which could damage your eyes at high intensities. (I did not look at the sun; I looked at the filter transmission curves.)

Maroon Flummoxed Catfish

I came across this blurb from Jordan Peterson the other day.

https://youtu.be/C3fy0RYpU8Q

It was extremely useful for me on clearing up that lingering distinction between knowledge and wisdom.

Also I might recommend our host's “The Way of the Golden Section.”

To my mind the discussion of the objective mind and the subjective mind is key.

To me the objective mind, centered in our brains, is the part that is obsessed with knowledge.

The subjective mind, centered in the solar plexus or heart center, is the part that responds to wisdom.

I lacked the language to describe this before but being heart centered and leading with empathy and compassion makes me feel more wise. For example it prevents me from putting my foot in my mouth for denouncing someone else making a different choice than I have when I haven’t paid attention to their circumstances and why they might have made that choice.

Not assuming moral superiority as inherent to intelligence is an important key. These are distinct areas.

“A man needs a skill or trade otherwise he will become corrupt.”

He also had a quote in there:

“Spinoza, who laid out his principles for political science thusly:
That I might investigate the subject matter of this science with the same freedom of spirit we generally use in mathematics, I have labored carefully not to mock, lament, or execrate human actions, but to understand them; and to this end I have looked upon passions such as love, hatred, anger, envy, ambition, pity, and other perturbations of the mind, not in the light of vices of human nature, but as properties just as pertinent to it as are heat, cold, storm, thunder, and the like to the nature of the atmosphere.”

Sophiaology the logic of wisdom
What it is?
How we find it?
What we do with it?

I’m just a forklift mechanic.

Wouldn’t it be great if people said I’m just a phd?

Wouldn’t it be great if more people said things like “with all the medical knowledge I’ve accrued working pipelines.” A.k.a. None.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As someone who also suffers from this, allow me to share a few things that I (think!) are helping me:

1) Building on Anonymous above, another Jordan Peterson insight I have found helpful is the idea that attention is a more important virtue than knowledge/intelligence - learning to pay attention to things that are not mainly about knowledge/information (like your breathing, or what plants and animals around you are doing, or the underlying emotional causes behind your rationaliation, etc) can really shift your perspective.

2) Speaking of paying attention to non-intellectual things, lately I've been getting a lot out of doing handcrafts and trying to use them as a spiritual exercise. Whatever you do, physical materials tend to assert their particularity in a way that deflates your easy abstractions real quick.

3) Though a rather intellectualized approach, the habits of thought Stoicism teaches are useful for becoming more self-aware, and can be turned to things like "am I getting caught up in gathering information rather than figuring out something practical?"

4) If your particular flavor of fetishization of knowledge/information is anything like mine, you might find yourself compulsively collecting sources of knowledge (books, links, articles, and so forth). I have found it helpful to adopt habits that focus on actually doing something with the sources of knowledge (read the books I already have, hand write notes on them, write a blog post or the like). Gathering and sorting information feels a lot like making progress, but it very likely is not unless you are doing something with it consistently.

Hope these help, and good luck!
Jeff

However, I sometimes seem to be able to sense these same substances in the environment itself, especially what Gurdjieff labeled “animal magnetism” or re 96 (which should be a product of breath). Is this animal magnetism actually in the environment? And is it a product of the Sun, stars, or planets?

Thanks for your help,
E.S.

replies.title: Re: Astral Light
replies.content: I'm not very familiar with Gurdjieff's teachings, so you'll have to adapt this by way of your own knowledge. The astral light is the substance of the astral plane, and animal magnetism is one name for the substance of the etheric plane, the way that physical matter is the substance of the material plane. The astral light is in fact generated by every star, including the sun, and reflected by planets; animal magnetism is the subtle principle behind breath; and yes, the astral light and animal magnetism are present in the environment -- the world consists of multiple planes of being, of which the material, etheric, and astral planes are the three lowest or densest.

replies.title: Re: Astral Light
replies.content:
Thank You, My Friend,
Yes, that dovetails perfectly with what I understand about Gurdjieffianism.
The human has three intakes of food, to Gurdjieff; two of which — edible food and breath — both produce the hydrogen 96. My feeling is that edible food produces bodily animal magnetism (fa 96) on the etheric plane, and breath produces internal Astral Light (re 96) on the Astral.
Gurdjieff would likely line up those three foods alongside his Ray of Creation, with edible food to Earth, and breath to All Planets. Impressions, the third food, would line up conjointly with the Sun, I would guess.

Thanks again for your knowledge, and Take Care!

comments.title: Sensitivity to external influences
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

This was a spiritually significant week. I noticed that the eclipse was going to have an impact on several of my loved ones (based on their birth charts), and was able to anticipate and navigate emotional situations that would have taken me off guard in the past. Thank you for pointing us in the right direction for this astrological event last week.

As one develops in occult studies, does one become more sensitive—or at least aware—of attempts to use magic or persuasion to control you?

Some context: For the last year, I’ve been working remotely for a financial institution. Last Wednesday I had to go into the office for the first time, and had a weird reaction.

It was clear to me that, within the space, “wills” outside myself were trying to control my consciousness. The walls were plastered with “motivational,” “inclusive,” phrases about every thing from covid 19 to diversity, to the benefits of walking up the stairs. The lunch room had TVs blaring the daily 2 minutes hate about the current thing. It was oppressive!

I had been in spaces like that before, but never felt psychically attacked. When I work from home I surround myself with books and beauty. I pray, I do banishing rituals, I take breaks to tend to my garden. I’ve worked hard to cultivate good vibes in my house.

As some one who’s been studying magic for just shy of a year… Is this what it will be like for me from now on? Being all too aware of pressures exerted on oneself by crappy corporate magic?

For a long time, I’ve been aware of the culture industry’s ability to induce emotional states, desires, and certain senses of self within an audience.. but never before have I felt it so acutely.

Is this just overreacting to being in a shared space after years of living and working in my own carefully cultivated environment? Is there anything that I can do to shield myself against these outside influences?

Thanks for your help!

replies.title: Re: Sensitivity to external influences
replies.content: Yes, this is a common experience, and it's one of the reasons occultists tend to avoid toxic spaces like the one you describe. Regular banishings and protective amulets can help, but eventually the best response is to go away.
